sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Syncope, also referred to as transient loss of consciousness (T-LOC), is defined as a rapid, self-limited loss of consciousness due to global cerebral hypoperfusion, resulting in spontaneous and complete recovery after the event. Syncope is a common prehospital presentation.
Presyncope describes symptoms such as dizziness, light-headedness, or weakness that may precede syncope but do not result in actual loss of consciousness.
Patients may describe syncopal episodes using terms such as “faint,” “black out,” “collapse,” or “passing out.” Syncope can be classified into the following major categories:
- Orthostatic syncope: Occurs secondary to postural changes, typically due to a failure of compensatory mechanisms to maintain blood pressure upon standing
- Reflex (neurally mediated) syncope: Results from an inappropriate autonomic nervous system response to a trigger or stimulus (e.g., vasovagal, situational, carotid sinus hypersensitivity)
- Cardiac syncope: Caused by a reduction in cardiac output due to arrhythmias or structural heart disease, leading to global cerebral hypoperfusion
- PLUS: SYNCOPE WITH additional provisional diagnosis i.e., syncope plus shortness of breath (PE), syncope plus abdominal or flank pain (AAA), syncope plus severe headache (Subarachnoid)

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden, brief loss of consciousness with spontaneous return to baseline
- Rapid onset and short duration (typically less than a minute)
- Loss of postural tone (patient may fall)
- Prodromal symptoms: Light-headedness, dizziness, nausea, sweating, visual changes, or palpitations
- Post-event: Brief confusion or disorientation is common; most patients recover quickly and completely
- May complain of pain
- Injuries: May occur due to fall (e.g., head trauma, fractures)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- A 12 lead ECG should be acquired in most patients unless an alternative obvious benign cause is clear and transport would be delayed
- Sustained unconsciousness is not consistent with simple syncope; if the patient remains unresponsive, treat as an alternative or additional diagnosis
- Syncope while supine, during exertion, or with chest pain, dyspnoea, severe headache, palpitations, abnormal vitals, or focal neurology should raise concern for a serious cause
- Consider cardiac arrhythmia, haemorrhage, orthostatic hypotension, hypoglycaemia, seizure, stroke or TIA, pulmonary embolus, aortic catastrophe, and non-fatal strangulation where relevant
- Patients with first-episode syncope, recurrent unexplained syncope, injury, or poor recovery warrant a low threshold for transport and senior review

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and correct immediate threats
- If still unconscious, manage the airway and place laterally unless another cause or injury requires a different approach
- If conscious, place supine initially and reassess symptoms and perfusion
- Administer oxygen only if indicated
- Record vital signs, especially blood pressure, heart rate, blood glucose, and conscious state
- Treat hypoglycaemia or other obvious reversible causes where identified
- Obtain rhythm assessment and focused secondary survey, including neurological and injury assessment
- Seek early senior support if recovery is incomplete, the cause is unclear, or red flags are present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG where feasible
- Treat dysrhythmia or other identified causes in line with the relevant condition-specific guideline
- Consider vascular access if recurrent syncope, abnormal vitals, trauma, or another serious cause is suspected
- Consider point-of-care blood testing where available and clinically useful
- Consider IV fluids if hypovolaemia, orthostatic hypotension, or sepsis is suspected
- Consider IV glucose only if hypoglycaemia is present or strongly suspected
- Provide analgesia or antiemetic therapy only when clinically indicated and not distracting from the underlying cause

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- The initial evaluation of patients with syncope should be focused on the early identification and management of serious and life-threatening non-cardiac or cardiac causes
- Non-lethal strangulation in domestic and family abuse may be a cause of unexplained syncope
- Determining the cause of a syncope can be complex in some patients and clinicians should have a low threshold for referring these patients for further medical review
- For some possible causes, consider AEIOUTIPS
- Alcohol, Arrhythmia, Epilepsy, Electrolytes, Insulin, Overdose, Oxygen, Opiates, Trauma, Temperature, Infection, Poisons, Psychiatric, Stroke, Shock, Syncope
- Patient Education: Advise on prevention of recurrence (e.g., avoid triggers, stay hydrated, rise slowly from sitting/lying)
